responseSet.title: When Does a Theory Stop Being Itself?: Commentary on Susan H. Sands's Paper
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

When Does a Theory Stop Being Itself?: Commentary on Susan H. Sands's Paper

Margaret Crastnopol PHD

Margaret Crastnopol

515 28th Avenue East
Seattle, WA 98112

Margaret Crastnopol  

It is a pleasure to read a constructively comparative paper, particularly when the author writes as an insider—from the “empathic-introspective position” of not only one, but both perspectives under scrutiny. Sands, a self-described relational clinician “whose work is grounded in self psychology,” demonstrates a good feel for the nuances of working within the frameworks of both self psychology and the relational orientation. She tellingly describes projective identification as the analyst's visceral experience of the patient, an “emotional exchange of body fluids,” which gratifies the patient's selfobject wish to be received and understood from the inside out. Sands writes convincingly about how and why the self-psychological lens “obscures” the role of these countertransferential analyst-patient communications in the analytic process.

I would amplify her sensible observation that self psychology “brings its own transference—its own organizing principles—to the psychoanalytic situation and thus creates its own reality” by noting that the same is certainly true of each of our theories—and the point bears restating.

Sands concludes that projective identification could be comfortably assimilated into the theoretical web of self-psychological constructs by being considered a function of a particular selfobject developmental need to “communicate certain unformulated affective experience through the other's experience, in order to explore vicariously and integrate intolerable aspects of self.” I disagree with this conclusion.

Dr. Crastnopol is Associate Director and Director of Curriculum, Northwest Center for Psychoanalysis, Seattle, WA. She is also a Clinical Instructor, Department of Psychology and Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, University of Washington.

© 1997 The Analytic Press

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Untying the Relational Knot: Commentary on Kenneth A. Frank's Paper

Emmanuel Kaftal PHD

Emmanuel Kaftal

559 Upper Boulevard
Ridgewood, NJ 07450

Emmanuel Kaftal  

The postclassical psychoanalyst is an uncertain creature, inhabiting an environment of shifting clinical and theoretical perspectives. To a large degree, this uncertainty is the result of the waning of the classical “archeological” model of the mind and the belief that analysts could and should reconstruct only those things that “existed” in the patient. A consensus is emerging among analysts that such an objective task is not possible. Most analysts know that they shape and influence what the see in the analytic work, and they try to understand their contribution.

Relational theorists generally attempt to celebrate this mutual influence and, to various degrees, the uncertainty which it raises. In one way or another, they argue that self and other are the product of interaction—and that because clinical work allows us to see this mutual shaping in process, it enriches our understanding of therapeutic action. But the nature of this mutual influence and the treatment techniques that would most usefully harness it are controversial. Do patient and analyst find each other? Or do they invent each other? Or is it, as Winnicott suggested, for analyst and patient that finding and inventing the other exist in a paradoxical relationship? How much time and focus should the analytic couple spend on understanding each person's influence on each other? Is the transference-countertransference a silent environment that performs essential psychological functions or is its clarification the only truly mutative activity?

Emmanuel Kaftal, Ph.D. is Supervisor in the New York University postdoctoral program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. He is also a Supervisor and Faculty at the New Jersey Institute for Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy, the Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy Study Center, the National Institute for the Psychotherapies, and the Institute for Contemporary Psychotherapy.

responseSet.title: Reply to Commentaries
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Commentaries

Kenneth Frank PHD

Kenneth Frank

330 West 58th Street, # 200
New York, NY 10019

Kenneth Frank  

I thank the commentators for their thoughtful reactions. I will take this opportunity to clarify misunderstandings, elaborate aspects of my position, and engage our differences. Given space limitations, I will not attempt a point-by-point reply but will respond to major themes. Points raised by one commentator may therefore be addressed in my responses to others.

Aron: The Problem of Analytic Anonymity

Although our differences are emphasized in this dialogue, as I have pointed out, there are many more similarities than differences between Lewis Aron's position and mine. Indeed, Aron's original views on mutuality have influenced my own thinking. Yet Aron disagrees with my assertion that anonymity is inimical to a thoroughgoing application of a two-person view. To my way of thinking, the concept of analytic anonymity is inherently problematic in relational models. It is a stance that was developed to facilitate scrutiny of the analysand's mental content without regard for the existence of the other person in the room and, consequently, for the role that interaction plays in the therapeutic action. An anonymous stance is intended to remove, or at least to reduce, the contribution of the analyst as an individual in the psychoanalytic situation. Thereby, it contributes to the very essence of the “one-person” psychoanalytic situation, emphasizing exploration of intrapsychic processes and downplaying the role of the interpersonal. While supporting the patient's blind acceptance of the analyst's objectivity and authority, anonymity promotes “distortion” qua distortion.

© 1997 The Analytic Press

responseSet.title: Working Through the Question of the Phallus to the Other Side: Commentary on Bracha Lichtenberg-Ettinger's Paper
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Working Through the Question of the Phallus to the Other Side: Commentary on Bracha Lichtenberg-Ettinger's Paper

Kareen Malone PHD

Kareen Malone

1500 Maple Street
Carrollton, GA 30118

Kareen Malone  

The work of Bracha Lichtenberg-Ettinger implicates psychoanalysis as a political, clinical, and aesthetic project and draws theoretically from each of these domains. As part of her challenge to psychoanalysis, Lichtenberg-Ettinger questions the Lacanian and Freudian preoccupation with the phallus. She queries the degree to which the psychical amplification of the phallus remains blindly tied to the penis and a masculine incarnation. She supplements this phallogocentric positioning with the concept of a matrixial subjectivity grounded in the intrauterine coexistence of mother and child. The womb and its possibilities indicate another relationship between the I and the non-I based on forms of relating that do not revolve around the dichotomy of exclusion and merger. As part of her critique of phallogocentrism, the author repudiates the absoluteness of a phallic presence versus castrated absence and the binaries in conceptualization to which this configuration leads.

The following commentary is deeply in sympathy with this project and appreciates the great reach of Lichtenberg-Ettinger project. Her project requires great care, however, as it tries to redefine formations of subjectivity through the Real of bodily difference. Further, it is not fully clear how matrixial subjectivity is situated with respect to certain questions raised by the phallus. More specifically, if the phallus represents a moment that structures one's relationship to speaking and to repression, a clearer articulation of the place of these moments in a matrixial subjectivity might create a more productive dialogue with the most innovative aspects of Lacanian psychoanalysis.

Kareen Ror Malone, Ph.D. is Associate Professor of Psychology and Faculty in the Women's Studies Program at State University of West Georgia.

© 1997 The Analytic Press

responseSet.title: Protein or Foreign Body?: Reply to Commentaries
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Protein or Foreign Body?: Reply to Commentaries

Susan Sands PHD

Susan Sands

1664 Solano Avenue
Berkeley, CA 94707

Susan Sands  

I feel fortunate to have received two such thoughtful, stimulating commentaries. Each has helped me clarify my position. Bacal's expansive account has helped me think more deeply about my own part in the “projective identificatory” process, and Crastnopol's incisive critique has helped me clarify where self psychology begins and leaves off in my own work.

Can Self Psychology's Theoretical Framework Support “Projective Identification”?

What of Crastnopol's most damning contention that self psychology does not possess an appropriate underlying theoretical framework to support the “borrowed concept” of projective identification? In the first place, I am not trying to “import” a concept from one theory to another. I am attempting to describe a particular phenomenon that happens in all forms of therapy—self-psychological, Kleinian, interpersonal, and otherwise. I am starting with the phenomenon and trying to find more self psychology-near language to explain it—not seeking to graft the Kleinian explanation whole onto self-psychological theory. Indeed, in the section in which I discuss the phenomenon as a particular selfobject need, I deliberately do not use the term “projective identification,” and here I will most often refer to this phenomenon as “experiencing-through-the-other.”

© 1997 The Analytic Press

responseSet.title: Anonymity, Desire, and Authenticity in the Analytic Relationship: Commentary on Kenneth A. Frank's Paper
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Anonymity, Desire, and Authenticity in the Analytic Relationship: Commentary on Kenneth A. Frank's Paper

Margaret Crastnopol PHD

Margaret Crastnopol

515 28th Avenue East
Seattle, WA 98112

Margaret Crastnopol  

Kenneth Frank addresses head-on the significance of something that is a moment-to-moment reality of our clinical lives—our unwitting self-revelation to our patients. He articulates the varieties and range of the analyst's advertent and inadvertent self-expression. Frank convincingly demonstrates how the joint exploration of the analyst's “personhood” or individuality can become a productive part—not just an unavoidable epiphenomenon—of the analytic endeavor.

Through his clinical illustration, Frank draws our attention to a particular type of self-revelation, in which the analyst impulsively reveals—or does not avoid exposing—an aspect of his or her personality that is potentially “undesirable.” The analyst may reveal unresolved feelings of frustration and competitiveness, as in the author's vignette, or some other personal quality or unresolved difficulty that stimulates aspects of the patient's negative transference (that is, the patient feels hurt, angered, or damaged by this aspect of the analyst). Such a revelation can be troublesome to the treatment process, especially if it is not properly and collaboratively explored. As analysts we are too often loathe to consider how our weaknesses, so tenacious that they may have weathered years of training analysis and supervision, are known to our patients and influence their work with us. We need to be more courageous in examining the particular analytic moment when analyst and patient together confront such things.

Margaret Crastnopol, Ph.D. is the Associate Director of the Northwest Center for Psychoanalysis in Seattle, Washington. She is a supervisor of psychotherapy and on the teaching faculty of the William Alanson White Institute.

© 1997 The Analytic Press

responseSet.title: Irreparable Objects—When There's Nothing to Mend: Commentary on Paper by Anne Alvarez
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Irreparable Objects—When There's Nothing to Mend: Commentary on Paper by Anne Alvarez

Elsa First MA

Elsa First

300 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024

Elsa First  

It is a privilege to discuss Anne Alvarez, for her work is one of the growing points of psychoanalysis today. Her writing has a remarkable immediacy. It fits with her clinical style, which favors those moments where something is discovered between child patient and therapist because they could both bear to know it at the same time. Inviting us to accompany her as she thinks, she allows herself to use all sorts of knowledge at once: naturalistic infant-parent observation, recent infant research, countertransference experiences with the most painfully disturbed children, Kleinian psychoanalysis, and her long experience in the Autism Workshop at the Tavistock Clinic, which allows her to ask what ordinary experiences in the development of relatedness (such as reaching out and grasping) are missing in the most shut-down children. Readers of her recent book, Live Company (Alvarez, 1992), a summation of her rich and generative work to that point, will know how her work with autism helped her notice and articulate small but significant increments in the growth of personhood in all sorts of children. In this new piece she looks back, with moving candor, on early case notes to study the implicit assumptions of the way she was taught and to figure out how far she has come. Her writing is so sensible and evocative and quietly brilliant, new readers may find that it goes down almost too easily. She needs to be re-read, I find, to appreciate her originality.

Elsa First is an Associate Clinical Professor in the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. She is a Training and Supervising Analyst and a faculty member at the New York Freudian Society.

© 1997 The Analytic Press

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Judith Rustin's Paper on Infancy, Agency, and Intersubjectivity

Carolyn Clement PHD

Carolyn Clement

210 West 90th Street, #8J
New York, NY 10024

Carolyn Clement  

Judith Rustin makes a creative argument for the application to the analytic situation of formal interaction principles and developmental constructs derived from mother-infant research. After briefly summarizing her interesting synthesis of the three major concerns that inform her paper—mother-infant interaction models, intersubjectivity in psychoanalysis, and her view of the sense of agency as the variable linking early development in the mother-infant dyad to change or growth in the analytic situation—I would like then to extend her discussion of the concept of agency and the nature of the therapeutic process as reflected in her case examples. Especially, I would encourge greater consideration of the patient's role in shaping the process and of the analyst's role as participating subject. While Rustin in her introductory and summary statements asserts that both participants are coconstructing the ongoing process, the case material underplays the patient's active role (both in self-regulation and in a purposeful, self-initiated impact on, or use of the analyst), and the analyst's countertransferential (or self-regulatory) activity. These limitations are not inherent in the mother-infant interaction models, in the model of intersubjectivity proposed by Stolorow and his colleagues, (Stolorow, Brandchaft, and Atwood, 1987) or in other self psychology paradigms. I expect that Rustin would argue that the issues I raise are reflected implicitly in her paper and I might agree. Yet I think they can be spelled out in a way that enriches her arguments and the material she presents.

Rustin focuses on the seminal work of Louis Sander, who applies the model of biological “system” to the mother-infant dyad.

Dr. Clement is an Adjunct Clinical Suprvisor at the Ferkauf Graduate School, Yeshiva University, and a faculty member at the National Institute for the Psychotherapies.

© 1997 The Analytic Press

“Once Upon a Time …” Is a Good Start, But Once Is Not Enough: Commentary on Paper by Beatriz Priel

Stephen Friedlander FAClinP

Stephen Friedlander

6025 Brookvale Lane, #120
Knoxville, TN 37919
KnowYrself@aol.com

Stephen Friedlander  

“Once passed, time leaves the imprint of its teeth …

(Lestienne, 1995, p. vii)

Patients sometimes complain about the strangeness of psychoanalysis—unusual silences, unfamiliar questions, and unexpected observations by the analyst, even the analyst's close attention and neutrality. Strangeness like this can disturb or shock, but we persist with our method because life itself is stranger than ordinary ideas convey. Analysis reveals that even the most patently conventional, ostensibly rational projects hide threads of idiosyncrasy and irrationality. We see that “meaningless” experiences—symptoms, slips, and dreams—are cunningly disguised commentaries on the relation between the analysand's early life and contemporary reality. This complex temporality is implicitly expressed in actions toward the analyst and in thoughts and feelings about the analyst. Strangeness, one might say, is our stock in trade, and we are specialists in pointing it out.

Dr. Priel uses temporality to interpret a familiar sort of clinical case. I suppose that her strategem may be uncomfortably exotic for some readers, but I believe that analysts need a certain estrangement from the familiar just as much as analysands do. We need to be comfortable with strangeness for two purposes: (1) to infuse our professional effort with creative passion and (2)

Dr. Friedlander is a member of the faculty and Director of Studies at the Institute for Psychoanalytic Education and Training, New York City, and Special Member of the Instituto de Investigacion en Psicologia Clinica y Social, A.C., Mexico City. Editor of Confronting the Challenges to Psychoanalysis, he is a past president of the International Federation for Psychoanalytic Education.

[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Commentary

Beatriz Priel PHD

Beatriz Priel

Beer-Sheva 84105
Israel
FAX: 07-6900046
email: bpriel@bgumail.bgu.ac.il

Beatriz Priel  

“We tend to think that where we are is where it's at.”

Levenson (1972)

I would like to begin by thanking Dr. Friedlander for his very thoughtful and challenging commentaries on “Time and Self.” He read this paper using a deconstructive-postmodern approach and questioned the relations between theory making and clinical practice. I shall reply to the theory/practice comment first and then elaborate on the position of “Time and Self” vis à vis deconstruction and postmodernist psychoanalysis.

Friedlander focuses on temporality in order to disentangle the relationships between theory and praxis in Mr. R's case: did theory precede and affect the analytic dialogue, or the other way round? As Friedlander rightly comments, however, “time in psychoanalysis is not linear” and therefore the temporal flow between in-session intersubjective dialogue and between-sessions theoretical reflection is nonlinear as well. Moreover, these two modalities juxtapose in very complex ways, with practice affecting and being affected by theory, both prospectively and retrospectively. The continuous conscious and unconscious transactions between theoretical reflection and clinical dialogue contribute to the analytical process insofar as they facilitate listening and open a broader range of possibilities. When our listening constrains itself to what is already known (our theories for instance), however, we are probably blocking the process. The alternative (no theory?) is neither recommendable nor possible.

In the context of the relations between theory and praxis, Friedlander focuses on the patient's relation to interpretation (“to what extent the patient understood [or even needed to understand] the role of temporality …”).

A Linguistic Contribution to Psychoanalysis: The Concept of Performative Contradictions

Leston Havens MD

Leston Havens

151 Brattle Street
Cambridge, MA 02138

Leston Havens  

Linguistic philosophy provides conceptual tools by which psychoanalysis can redefine its conditions of productive discourse. In particular, the idea of performative contradictions permits ready noting of obstructions to that discourse.

The present paper means to develop an adequate vocabulary defining the conditions of what is essentially analytic and the positive events accompanying them. These conditions can be seen as systematic substitutions for such recommendations as anonymity and neutrality.

responseSet.title: Response to Review
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Review

Neil Altman PHD

Neil Altman

127 W. 79th St. #3
New York, N.Y. 10024

Neil Altman  

I very much appreciate Dr. Botticelli's thoughtful response to my book. The review has enhanced my own understanding of the issues about which I have written and has stimulated me to carry my thinking considerably further.

Botticelli's review speaks to a tension felt by many psychoanalytically inclined therapists who have, at various points in their lives, been social activists or critically concerned with social issues. How does one bring psychological and social reality into relation with each other, on the conceptual level, and on the level of action? On the conceptual level my ideal is a model that locates the psychological in the social and the social in the psychological. Such a formulation establishes a tension between the integrity of each domain and the inextricability of each from the other. I agree with Boticelli that psychological reductionism fails to recognize the integrity of the social realm; social reductionism does the same with respect to the psychological realm. At the other extreme, an overemphasis on the integrity of each domain leads to viewing the psychological and the social as fundamentally separate in a way that artificially divides up reality. Recognizing that the social and the psychological are inextricably intertwined is different from having one domain colonize the other. Analogously, relational psychoanalysis has come to view the intrapsychic as interpersonal and the interpersonal as intrapsychic. Each domain is viewed as having its unique characteristics, yet the two are viewed as inseparable.

Dr. Altman is Co-Chair of the Relational Orientation, Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, New York University, and is an Associate Editor of this journal.

responseSet.title: On Subjective Organizations Toward a Theory of Subject Relations
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Subjective Organizations Toward a Theory of Subject Relations

Roger Kennedy MD

Roger Kennedy

1 Ham Common
Richmond, Surrey TW107JF
United Kingdom
roger@kennedy.prestel.co.uk

Roger Kennedy  

This essay puts forward for discussion a “subject relations” theory of psychoanalysis. It is argued that the term subject captures a basic aspect of the human situation, that we are both subject “of” and subject “to” various phenomena. It is argued that psychoanalytic subjectivity implies being in several different positions or viewpoints in relation to others. The subject moves between positions within the subjective organization. The paper tackles such basic issues as the way such subjective organizations both arise and are sustained, how unity is maintained but also undermined. The essential role of social structure in the subjective organization is discussed. The role of objects in a subject relations theory is considered. In addition, various transference states are described that could reflect particular subjective organizations. Lastly, there is a brief consideration of the role of the analyst's subjectivity in the analytic relationship.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Beyond/Outside Gender Dichotomies—Introduction: New Forms of Constituting Subjectivity and Difference

Adrienne Harris PHD

Adrienne Harris

80 University Place, 5th floor
New York City, NY 10003

Adrienne Harris  

The contemporary project of understanding and deconstructing gender could be described as the tracing of dialectical connections of gender experience to social formations, to intrapsychic life, and to the life of the body. By dialectical, I mean that we see gender as emergent from relationships, embedded in cultural and social forms and that body ego and psychic structure coconstruct each other. The relational perspective in particular has tried to eschew or minimize the search for origins and essential bedrock structures that dictate and form particular psychological or interpersonal outcomes.

Another strong feature of the gender theory that has been developed in this journal (Benjamin, 1991; Dimen, 1991; Goldner, 1991; Kaftal, 1991; Aron, 1995) and elsewhere (Benjamin, 1988; Flax, 1990; Harris, 1991, 1996; Chodorow, 1992, 1994; Mitchell, 1996) has been a critical attention to the rigidity and constrictions in so much dichotomizing in gender analysis. The brutality of these oppositions, both as they inform theory and as they live painfully in analytic dyads and in individual lives, is of increasing concern. This perspective is by no means unique to the relational school. There is a strong and sophisticated critique of phallic monism from within the neo-Freudian tradition in France (Torok, 1971, Chasseguet-Smirgel, 1971; McDougall, 1989) and from the philosophic feminism of Irigaray (1985).

Adrienne Harris, Ph.D. is a faculty member and supervisor at the New York University Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. She is an Associate editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues and of a new journal, Gender and Psychoanalysis. She has coedited, with Lewis Aron, The Legacy of Sandor Ferenczi and has written on topics of gender, language theory, and psychoanalytic feminism.

© 1997 The Analytic Press

responseSet.title: On the Reluctance to Sanction Self-Disclosure: Commentary on Kenneth A. Frank's Paper
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Reluctance to Sanction Self-Disclosure: Commentary on Kenneth A. Frank's Paper

Karen Maroda, PHD

Karen Maroda,

611 East Wisconsin Avenue, #540
Milwaukee, WI 53202

Karen Maroda,  

Dr. Frank's paper portrays a variety of ideas concerning self-disclosure. Such a variety, in fact, that I found myself forgetting exactly what this paper was about. Frank argues for a two-person perspective, for greater mutuality, for admitting the impact of conscious and unconscious communications, for combining intrapsychic and interpersonal exploration, and for the value of deliberate (as opposed to inadvertent) disclosure. At the same time, he warns against the pitfalls of deliberate disclosure. When all is said and done, he appears to favor therapist self-disclosure as part of a technical approach within a two-person theoretical stance.

I think the strongest point of this paper comes when Frank says that we remain reluctant to alter psychoanalytic technique to accommodate our new two-person theories, preferring the safety of trying to fit one-person pegs into two-person holes.

I agree with him that finding the appropriate role for self-disclosure is the bedrock of any new technical approach. Once we commit ourselves to a more mutual, two-person approach (whether we call it interpersonal, relational, intersubjective, or something else), we either participate more fully in the psychoanalytic encounter or we fail to meet the requirements of our own re-definition of the analytic situation. Frank recommends the selective use of countertransference (including making inadvertent disclosures more manifest and therefore within the conscious control of the analyst), but laments the lack of support for this in the literature.

Dr. Maroda is Assistant Professor of Psychiatry at the Medical College of Wisconsin. She is in private practice in Milwaukee, WI.

© 1997 The Analytic Press

[[RunningHead]]

Self-Disclosure and the Interactive Matrix: Commentary on Kenneth A. Frank's Paper

Lewis Aron PHD

Lewis Aron

243 West End Avenue, #310
New York, NY 10023

Lewis Aron  

In this timely article, Kenneth Frank makes use of my work on interpretation and subjectivity as a foil to set off and highlight his own position in contrast to mine. Although there are some ways in which I feel misunderstood by Frank, it is nevertheless intensely gratifying to think that my previous contributions have been useful in furthering our collective conversation, an attempt to formulate a relational approach to questions of psychoanalytic technique, particularly the issue of self-disclosure.

I do believe that there is a genuine difference between Frank and myself, but I do not think that it has so much to do with our attitudes toward or belief in the efficacy of self-disclosure, as I too believe that self-disclosures may at times be used quite productively. The difference between us lies in our divergent approaches to formulating a relational theory of psychoanalytic technique. Frank suggests that “analytic anonymity is not an integral element of two-person theories, but is inimical to them.” Instead of anonymity, he recommends “a process that utilizes countertransference directly through collaborative attempts to explicate specific interactions with the patient.” Frank is advocating a specific technical stance, namely, the active use of self-disclosure.

My emphasis, by way of contrast, has not been on either advocating or disparaging self-disclosure; rather, it has been on eschewing any singular technique as the optimal psychoanalytic method. The articles of mine that Frank cites (Aron, 1991, 1992)

Lewis Aron, Ph.D. ABPP is Clinical Professor and Supervisor at the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis and at the Adelphi University Derner Institute for Advanced Psychological Studies. He is an associate editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

responseSet.title: Infancy, Agency, and Intersubjectivity: A View of Therapeutic Action
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Infancy, Agency, and Intersubjectivity: A View of Therapeutic Action

Judith Rustin CSW

Judith Rustin

79 West 12th Street
New York, NY 10011

Judith Rustin  

This paper uses concepts from infant research to describe the therapeutic action of psychoanalysis. Louis Sander's model of bidirectional mutual influence implies that agency is a property of all living things. Studying sleep-wake cycles, Sander concludes that these are mutually regulated; the infant uses his state to guide his pole of the bidirectionality. It is in the “moment of meeting,” when the caregiving surround acknowledges the infant state in a way that “fits,” lies the beginnings of the infant's sense of agency. I suggest that one can make links between how a sense of agency develops in infancy and how this concept can be used in psychoanalytic treatment. Specifically, a bidirectional stance, a continuous attention to affect and state, and a systematic inquiry into how the patient is experiencing the analytic activity foster a sense of agency. Clinical examples illustrate the thesis.

responseSet.title: Beyond the Word in Psychoanalysis: The Unspoken Dialogue
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Beyond the Word in Psychoanalysis: The Unspoken Dialogue

Steven Knoblauch PHD

Steven Knoblauch

30 West 13th Street #3A
New York, NY 10011

Steven Knoblauch  

The practice of psychoanalysis, traditionally, has privileged verbal communication. In relational theory the therapeutic significance of unconscious, semantically unformulated, but nonverbally encoded communications has been recognized. While patients are understood to generate significant nonverbal material for use in the treatment process, similar nonverbal productions from analysts rarely have been acknowledged as useful for treatment. In this paper, I demonstrate a way to recognize and use the nonverbal productions of analysts as well as of analysands within the analytic process. A brief review is offered of contemporary authors who have conceptualized the importance of the nonverbal realm for psychoanalytic treatment. An approach is then described that builds on process conceptualizations derived from the infant research literature. The model, illustrated with two clinical examples, provides a way of conceptualizing “process contours” on a nonverbal level of exchange between analyst and analysand that becomes the medium for catalyzing the transition from presymbolic interactions to symbolic language. Working on this level, analyst and analysand coconstruct scenes from both the dreaded past and the hoped-for present/future, which, when reflected on in a joint process of narrative construction, provide a new context for accessing previously dissociated affective experience.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis as a Vocation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis as a Vocation

Jessica Benjamin PHD

Jessica Benjamin

228 West 22nd Street
New York, NY 10011

Jessica Benjamin  

Psychoanalysis may be seen as caught between two different trends of disenchanted modernity: rationalization, which leads to the framing of our work as a professional discipline subordinate to the dictates of instrumental rationality, and self-analysis, which frees us from the dictates of orthodoxy, inequality, and authority. But a further difficulty lies within the aspect of enlightenment, which has not only provided a greater role for our subjectivity but disguised relations of authority, conformity, and objectification in our work. Psychoanalysis has objectified the other while idealizing its knowledge as objective, has paradoxically denied the very subjectivity that must serve as the source of the analyst's knowledge. However, the reaction against this condition, which may tend to produce counter-ideals of not-knowing and mutuality, must also be carefully deconstructed. Differences in the meaning given by different schools to the use of the analyst's subjectivity suggest that pluralism will make new knowledge demands on psychoanalysts. Analytic training should include the development of critical abilities that help to meet these demands within a context of education as a collaborative, democratic process. Knowledge itself can be used homeopathically as an antidote to the old ideal of the knowing authority.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Two Quibbles: Commentary on Kenneth A. Frank's Paper

Stephen Mitchell PHD

Stephen Mitchell

251 W. 71st St.
New York, NY 10023

Stephen Mitchell  

Frank presents a well-reasoned exploration of the inevitability of the analyst's continual inadvertent self-revelations throughout the analytic process and well-argued position in favor of the analyst's conscientious self-scrutiny of his or her participation and openness to discuss that participation with the patient. In so doing, he is, as he suggests, expanding on some of the implications of the more general shift from a one-person to a two-person framework that has dominated postclassical psychoanalysis in recent decades. On the whole, I am persuaded by his argument and find little to quibble with. But finding little to quibble with is different from finding nothing to quibble with, and the quibbles seem worth noting.

First, there is a big difference between the inevitability of the analyst's deeply personal participation in the process, on one hand, and the therapeutic utility of that participation being talked about and made explicit, on the other. Of course, one can collapse these two issues by arguing, as do some interpersonalists, that the very goal of psychoanalysis is the clarification of the patient's interpersonal relations. If that is the case, then it would be hard to imagine any good reason for not making the analyst's experience of his or her own participation explicit. But I would not define the goal of psychoanalysis in those singular terms. There are patients who come into treatment searching for personal meaning, looking for a personal voice they have never had the opportunity to develop, tortured by their past, or their dreams, and so on.

Dr. Mitchell, Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues, is a Training and Supervising Analyst at the William Alanson White Institute and on the faculty of the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

[[RunningHead]]

Incognito or Not?: The Patient's Subjective Experience of the Analyst's Private Life

Margaret Crastnopol PHD

Margaret Crastnopol

515 28th Avenue East
Seattle, WA 98112

Margaret Crastnopol  

This paper examines the meaning for the patient of the analyst's personal life and personality which are ostensibly banished from the consulting room. The therapist has a not-always-so-secret “secret life” that the patient is supposed to “not know” about. Yet, more or less unconscious perceptions, impressions, and fantasies about extratherapeutic aspects of the analyst are omnipresent and significantly color the psychoanalytic enterprise.

Moreover, the analyst as a person generally plays a critical and underacknowledged role in the patient's experience of the endeavor. Constructing multiple overlapping images of the analyst and of the analytic relationship, the patient discovers himself or herself in the matrix of these relationships with various images of the analytic other. The analysand is motivated to make sense of the analyst as wholly as possible, the better to place into context the analyst's interventions. The patient's resulting view of the analyst's subjective experience acts as a lens that filters and subtly alters the meaning of the analyst's communications.

I illustrate these points by relating my work with a patient whose dreams uncannily picked up on a (consciously) unknown aspect of my private life—my having a handicapped son. The treatment thereafter centered on the patient's identification with my child (as someone

responseSet.title: Some Thoughts on the Use of Language in Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Thoughts on the Use of Language in Psychoanalysis

Thomas Ogden MD

Thomas Ogden

306 Laurel Street
San Francisco, CA 94118

Thomas Ogden  

In this paper I comment on several aspects of the way language is used in psychoanalysis. Language is viewed not simply as a “package” for carrying ideas and feelings, but as a medium in which thoughts and feelings are created. In the analytic setting, analyst and analysand are viewed as engaged in an effort to use language in a way that is adequate to the task of creating/conveying a sense of what it feels like for the patient to be human, to the extent that he is capable at a given moment (with particular emphasis on describing the leading anxiety that the analysand is experiencing).

The analyst strives to use language in a way that embodies the tension of forever struggling to generate meaning while at every step casting doubt on the meanings “arrived at” or “clarified.” Forms of lifelessness of analytic language are discussed with emphasis on those forms of linguistic deadness that derive from (1) the analyst's ideological attachment to a particular school of analytic thought and (2) the analyst's unconscious participation in an intersubjective construction jointly, but asymmetrically, generated by analyst and analysand (Ogden, 1994a, b, in press a, b).

Finally, I discuss the idea of effects being created in language and suggest that this phenomenon constitutes a principal medium through which unconscious meaning is communicated in the analytic setting. Such unconscious effects in language are generated at least as much through the way the patient (or analyst) is speaking as through what the patient (or analyst) is saying.

This paper does not represent an effort to apply analytic thinking to the field of literary studies. Instead, I hope to make a small contribution to an awareness of the life of words (and the life in words) that occurs in the analytic situation. Rather than attempting to look behind language, the effort here is to look into it.

Dr. Ogden is Codirector, Center for the Advanced Study of the Psychoses, San Francisco, CA; Supervising and Personal Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; and Faculty, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute.

Anne Alvarez M.A.C.P.

Anne Alvarez

45 Flask Walk
London NW3IHH
England

Anne Alvarez  

This paper distinguishes between the grammar of wishes in neurotic patients and the grammar of imperative needs in borderline patients. The treatment of a borderline psychotic boy 30 years ago was uninformed by subsequent developments in technique arising from the theory of projective identification as a communication rather than as an attack or a defense. Issues that need addressing in borderline patients, especially those who are children, include (a) developmental delay, (b) the distintion between defenses and overcomings in the paranoid position, (c) rectification fantasies of vengeance, and (d) rectification fantasies of justice and other moral imperatives.

Emanuel Berman PHD

Emanuel Berman

Haifa 31905, Israel
E-mail: RSPS360@UVM.HAIFA.AC.IL

Emanuel Berman  

The history of the stormy love affair between psychoanalysts and literature is explored, with an emphasis on their basic affinity and on the animosity created by analysts' reductionistic tendencies. Past studies of the dynamics of Robert Frost's emotional world and poetry are reviewed in this context. Ogden's sensitive reading of Frost's poems is portrayed as an example of the potentially mutual egalitarian encounter allowed by newer trends in psychoanalytic theory.

Before I built a wall I'd ask to know

What I was walling in or walling out,

And to whom I was like to give offense.

Something there is that doesn't love a wall,

That wants it down.

(“Mending Wall”; Frost, 1964, p. 47)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Role of the Analyst's Inadvertent Self-Revelations

Kenneth Frank, PHD

Kenneth Frank,

330 West 58 Street, # 200
New York, NY 10019

Kenneth Frank,  

Although the technique of countertransference-disclosure has received increasing attention, interest in the role of the analyst's inadvertent self-revelations has remained quite limited. Yet the analyst's inadvertent self-revelations are at the heart of the distinction between monadic and two-person views of the psychoanalytic situation. This paper extends the logical implications of a two-person perspective on the psychoanalytic situation. Because the analyst's inadvertent self-revelations are inevitable and continuous, they significantly shape the patient's experience of the relationship. The analytic process at times can be enhanced, therefore, by collaborative efforts to make explicit the inadvertent expressions of the analyst. Aspects of the analyst's individuality thereby become clarified, rather than obscured as in classical views. Dealing with the analyst's inadvertent self-revelations in an active, collaborative manner that is tempered by asymmetry elucidates the integration of interpersonal with intrapsychic aspects of transference and heightens the immediacy and directness of the analytic interaction. This approach also embraces the significance of the analyst's personal participation, authenticity, and interaction, which though crucial in two-person models, have been obscured by concerns with maintaining neutrality, anonymity, and abstinence, which epitomize the analyst's stance from the one-person perspective.

[[RunningHead]]

Mother-Infant Interaction Structures and Presymbolic Self- and Object Representations

Beatrice Beebe PHD , Frank Lachmann PHD and Joseph Jaffe MD

Joseph Jaffe

722 W. 168th St., #108
New York NY, 10032-2603

221 West 82nd Street, Apt. 11G
New York, NY 10024

722 W. 168th St.
New York, NY 10032-2603

Joseph Jaffe  

Using research on the purely social face-to-face exchange, we examine patterns of mother-infant interaction and their relevance for the presymbolic origins of self and object representations, focusing on the representation of inter-relatedness between self and object. Based on a dyadic systems view in which the system is defined by both self- and interactive-regulation processes, we argue that characteristic patterns of self and interactive regulation form early interaction structures, which provide an important basis for emerging self and object representations. What will be represented, presymbolically, is the dynamic interactive process itself, the interplay, as each partner influences the other from moment to moment. This is a dynamic, process view of “interactive” or “dyadic” representations. The argument that early interaction structures organize experience is based on a transformational model in which there are continuous transformations and restructurings, where development is in a constant state of active reorganization. To define the capacities on which a presymbolic representational capacity is based, we review the last decade's research on infant perception and memory, which has radically changed our concepts of representation. The interaction structures

Frank Lachmann, Ph.D. is Core Faculty, Institute for the Psychoanalytic Study of Subjectivity, and Training Analyst, Postgraduate Center for Mental Health.

Joseph Jaffe, M.D. is Professor of Clinical Psychiatry, College of Physicians and Surgeons, Columbia University, and Chief, Department of Communication Sciences, New York State Psychiatric Institute.

we describe illustrate the salience of arousal, affect, space, and time in the early organization of experience: (1) state transforming, the expectation that an arousal state can be transformed through the contribution of the partner; (2) facial mirroring, the expectation of matching and being matched in the direction of affective change; (3) disruption and repair, the expectation of degree of ease and rapidity of interactive repair following facial-visual mismatches; (4) “chase and dodge,” the expectation of the misregulation and derailment of spatial-orientation patterns, without repair; and (5) interpersonal timing, the expectation of degree of vocal rhythm matching.

Reply to Commentary

Bracha Lichtenberg-Ettinger PHD

Bracha Lichtenberg-Ettinger

Bracha Lichtenberg-Ettinger  

Iam deeply thankful to Kareen Malone for her attentive and passionate reading of my essay, which allows a dialectical approach to the issues at stake. I discussed elsewhere some of her specific concerns: Lacan's emphasis on the phallus as a structure (Lichtenberg-Ettinger, 1992, 1993a, b; 1996a) and likewise the priority of the matrix as a sense-revealing/producing apparatus (Lichtenberg-Ettinger, 1992, 1995a, 1996a), the possibility of “amicable relations with the Other” (Lichtenberg-Ettinger, 1995a, 1996b), the links of the coemerging I and non-I with phenomenology (Lichtenberg-Ettinger, 1995c, 1996b), and the differences and affinities between Lacan's objet a, and objects in other theories (Lichtenberg-Ettinger, 1995a, b). The difference between Malone's and my Lacanian perspective concerns the place of language: whereas Malone emphasizes its centrality and “the Other as the locus of the signifier,” I accentuate the unconscious beyond the chains of the signifiers. If indeed “the S1 … need not follow the route of the phallic function” we should question why it is still presented as “forming the kernel of the phallus,” thus allowing the phallus to define both its own proper scope and its beyond, whether as before (stage) or beside (structure).

Indeed, the challenge to feminine difference, as Malone so eloquently shows, had been, precisely, not just to exchange an organ (penis) and its image for another (womb), but to conceive of an alternative to the phallus in terms of structure, mechanism, functions, logic. Karen Rosica Psy.D.

Karen Rosica

300 Josephine St., #250
Denver, CO 80206

Karen Rosica  

Descriptions of clinical work tend toward the linear thus omitting the shifting intricacies of the known and the unknown inherent in the work. This essay, both in its form and its content, attempts to capture more closely the often kaleidoscopic complexity of the lived experience of the analytic process. The paper describes the emergence of multilayered transference—countertransference contributions to a single moment in clinical time.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Making It New Enough: Commentary on Paper by Altman

Adam Phillips

Adam Phillips

99 Talbot Road
London W11, England

Adam Phillips  

Just before this paper was first presented, I was teaching a short course on the Oedipus complex in our hospital department. Of course, from a psychoanalytic point of view, the idea of teaching the Oedipus complex is itself rather strange: After all, what else has one ever learned about, and yet how little one seems to know. What is learning about the Oedipus complex (hearing papers, doing clinical work, etc.) learning about? Do children learn about the Oedipus complex like they might learn to play basketball or checkers? Or is it something they keep bumping into: Every so often there is something they keenly want to do, and they find an obstacle there, which we call the Oedipus complex? Certainly, in Freud's view, if you don't get the oedipal story—this terrible joke of triangulation—you don't join the human community. You are left out. But perhaps we need to bear in mind, faced with what Freud called in a letter to Fliess “the riveting power of Oedipus Rex” (Masson, 1985) the part of ourselves that, like Oedipus, doesn't get it. By that I don't mean simply the part that doesn't want to believe it, or rebels against the pain of it, but the part of ourselves that is irresolvably perplexed by that fundamental drama of being included in something, only to be left out of it. Oedipus, like all of us, was from his point of view going through it for the first time. So what does knowing the story do to the story? Are psychoanalysts the people who know the story?

Adam Phillips was formerly Principal Child Psychotherapist at Charing Cross Hospital in London. He is the author, most recently, of Terrors and Experts and Monogamy.

Karen Maroda

611 East Wisconsin Avenue, #540
Milwaukee, WI 53202

Karen Maroda  

This article explores the possibilities for the relationship between various taboo heterosexual relationships and an underlying homosexual preference. The analytic literature has not explored the tendency for many suppressed or repressed homosexuals to marry in order to escape shame over their sexual feelings and to avoid the social rejection experienced by those who are openly gay. The hypothesis stated here is that persons who are unconsciously gay are more likely to incorporate an element of taboo in their heterosexual relationships. The taboo heterosexual relationship substitutes for the more verboten homosexual one and provides an element of “being different” that may provide greater sexual arousal than a conventional relationship.

Models of Learning and Psychoanalytic Traditions Can Reform Be Sustained in Psychoanalytic Training?

Jonathan Slavin PHD

Jonathan Slavin

Medford, MA 02155

Jonathan Slavin  

This paper presents a series of training vignettes which suggest that a subtext of defensiveness regarding exposure to our patients, fear of feelings, adherence to an unwritten set of rules, and a profound need to control our way of thinking and working pervades the way psychoanalysis is taught and practiced in many settings. The paper explores the way in which the basic culture of psychoanalysis, and the manner in which psychoanalysts are socialized, may make these kinds of episodes commonplace. The paper suggests that a paradigm shift that has been taking place in psychoanalytic clinical thinking has not yet affected the training process because of the danger that the new paradigm might undermine the safety, clarity, and ways of knowing that our traditional theories and clinical practices have provided.

Historical Legacies and Contemporary Innovation: Introduction to Symposium on Child Analysis Part I

Stephen Seligman DMH

Stephen Seligman

3667 Sacramento Street
San Francisco, CA 94118

Stephen Seligman  

As readers of this journal are well aware, North American psychoanalysis has been transformed in the last decade by the relational revision of analytic paradigms. Relational understandings have become essential in both theory and technique, with an intersubjective, developmentally oriented perspective gaining prominence at the center of current analytic controversies. But the integration of this transformation with child analysis has lagged far behind its effect on clinical work with adults and on theory in general. The publication of the following collection of articles in Psychoanalytic Dialogues points toward the promising prospect that this gap may be closing, such that the move toward a relationship-oriented psychoanalysis can now be allied with the varied strengths of the child analytic tradition.

That this has not happened sooner would appear to be a curious state of affairs. Historically, child analysis has been, arguably, the most fertile source of innovation in analytic thinking. Many of the leading attributes of the relational turn have long been at the center of child analytic theory and practice: the overall developmental perspective; the assertion of the importance of the earliest relationships and especially of mother-infant relationships; and, in regard to clinical theory, an affirmative attention to the value and meaning of clinical interaction and of relationships in general (Sabot, 1980).

Stephen Seligman, D.M.H. is Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Infant-Parent Program, University of California, San Francisco; Personal and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; and Faculty, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute.

My art practice has led me to rethink sexual difference within psychoanalytic theory. In the Lacanian classical reading of Freud, the feminine either is articulated via the model of male “castration” and paternal prohibition or is equated with pure bodily experience, whereas the phallus is considered as the only “signifier of signifiers” for sexual difference. In Lacan's late theory, “woman” escapes the phallus and has “supplementary” jouissance,1 but her evasion is still defined by its parameters. If “woman” is either an object of exchange or an inaccessible Other/Thing/objet a as absence, she may be such only inside an altogether phallic paradigm. But, seen through a prism that I call matrixial, “woman” digs an-other area of difference with its specific apparatus, processes, and functions.

In the matrixial model that hereby will be knitted in a non-linear way, the feminine/prenatal encounter is a non-phallic sex-difference issue primarily for women. I articulate it around the rapport with female bodily specificity in the Real (womb) as inside and/or outside, as in-forming an unconscious zone. Bion's conception of early, intersubjective

Dr. Lichtenberg-Ettinger is an artist and psychoanalyst practicing in Paris. She has had solo exhibitions in Israel Museum, Jerusalem: Museum of Modern Art, Oxford; Pori Museum, Finland; Russian Museum, St. Petersburg; Nouveau Musée and Musée de Calais, France and group exhibitions in Centre Pompidou, Paris; Stedelijk Museum, Amsterdam; ICA, Boston; Whitechapel, London and Tel Aviv Museum. Dr. Lichtenberg-Ettinger is the author or coauthor of several books on psychoanalysis and aesthetics.

Translations from J. A. Miller, J. Lacan, and others are, unless otherwise stated, by J. Simas and B. Lichtenberg-Ettinger.

This book was very interesting to read, and it stimulated my thinking. Sometimes, however, it made me wonder and filled me with surprise. In this paper, I give a reflection of my wondering, concentrating on two points.

First, I learned from this book, more strongly than I would have supposed, that in the United States there seems to exist a clear-cut dichotomy between two tendencies. The first is the “classical” one, also called the “Freudian” (but why it should be more Freudian than the other leaves us Europeans with a feeling of mystery), which, in the presentation of Aron, means ego-psychological or drive-discharge oriented (the two are taken together). The second tendency is (the “good”) one—relationship oriented. This dichotomy runs through the entire book, evidently historically, institutionally, and culturally determined. Such a dichotomy already presents some difficulties when trying to rethink psychoanalytic experiences in the European context. Adrienne Harris

80 University Place, 5th fl.

Adrienne Harris  

Empirical research on infant cognitive, perceptual, and social competence has had probably the most far-reaching impact on psychoanalytic ideas of any modern research program. It is interesting to remember that this domain of research owes its beginnings to psychoanalysis; it traces its origins to the psychoanalytic work (theory and practice) of Bowlby (1969), and others in the postwar period in Europe. In that social and political-economic crisis psychoanalysts (particularly in England) were in the forefront of efforts to repair the great disruption the war had onfamily life and the terrible impact of separations and parental loss on child development.

What we might call the entrepreneurial phase of infancy research was inaugurated in the 1960s in a series of important research programs whose impact on a general audience of psychologists and psychiatrists was profound. Church, Smith, and Murphy's (1973) edited collection marked as a crucial point in the dissemination of research findings on what infants could see, notice, remember, and attend to. Since that period, our view of infant life has been successively transformed, and this transformation affected on the working understanding of many subdisciplines in psychology. In the area of cognitive development, for example, infancy studies, in concert with other work have led to many revisions of early childhood cognitive capacity and have confirmed many of the Piagetian accounts of the unfolding of cognitive stages but placing many capacities, in their rudimentary forms, at earlier ages.

The empirical work, then, transformed psychoanalytic understanding of early experience, the linking or relay in that process being the highly influential work of Daniel Stern (1985).

The widening scope of psychoanalysis seems to shrink back at the point the poor or culturally different come into the picture. With few exceptions (e.g., Langer, 1989), these people have been seen as inappropriate for psychoanalytic therapy. The exclusion of people of lower socioeconomic status from psychoanalytic treatment is mirrored on the theoretical level by the neglect of the influence of social factors on the development of personality. Whether one thinks in terms of the Oedipus complex or selfobject experience, it is as if individual development unfolded in a near social vacuum.

With his new book, The Analyst in the Inner City: Race, Class, and Culture Through a Psychoanalytic Lens, Neil Altman means to challenge these tendencies of thought and practice. Altman argues that, just as psychoanalytic therapy aims to help the individual integrate those aspects of self that have been psychically disowned, much can be added to our collective understanding by bringing into psychoanalytic consideration those people who have traditionally been excluded by our theories and practices. Sweden

Arne Jemstedt  

In this commentary, Jacobson's exploration of Bollas's notions of “true self” and “idiom” is discussed. I underline Bollas's focus on unconscious processes in general and in the psychoanalytic relation in particular and—referring to Bion and Bollas—discuss the importance of the analyst's intuitive capacity in sensing these processes. Winnicott's and Bollas's formulations on the notion of true self/idiom are briefly reviewed and its unknowable quality is emphasized. While being in many respects appreciative of Jacobson's essay, I argue against his position of not considering idiom as something inherent and unique to each person, claiming that Jacobson's understanding of Bollas's notion sometimes is too concrete. I argue as well against Jacobson's view of Bollas as a “one-person theorist” and as holding a position leading to “a distancing of the social world.”

Johanna Tabin PHD

Johanna Tabin

162 Park Avenue
Glencoe, IL 60022

Johanna Tabin  

Beebe, Lachmann, and Jaffe build a case for the possibility that interactions between mothers and their babies of four months become encoded as permanent intrapsychic structures of the babies' brains. As part of doing so, they also detail a remarkable contribution to the technique of studying interpersonal behavior, micro time-series regression analysis. Through the efficacy of their technique, they establish that infants as young as at four months of age engage in a process of interaction in which the infant may both initiate and maintain the scenario. I will return to this finding and some of its implications. Because the authors place the finding in the context of relating early psychological experience to later personality, that seems the first matter to discuss.

Persistence of Encoded Patterns

The interesting review of the literature in the first half of the article supports the possibility that an interactive pattern encoded by the infant early on will persist through the several cortical reorganizations that characterize its maturing brain (Hebb, 1949; Metcalf, 1979). Specifically, Beebe and her colleagues (1997) point out that patterns of infant-mother attunement in early infancy correlate highly with the nature of the relationship when the infant is a year old.

Johanna Krout Tabin, Ph.D. is in private psychoanalytic practice in the Chicago area and is a member of the faculty and administrative board of the Chicago Center for Psychoanalysis. Her publications reflect a long-held interest in early development.

[[RunningHead]]

The Soul of Psychoanalysis in the Modern World: Reflections on the Work of Christopher Bollas

Lawrence Jacobson PHD

Lawrence Jacobson

14 East 4th Street, Ste. 608
New York, NY 10012

Lawrence Jacobson  

The paper begins with the claim that psychoanalysis faces a dilemma in locating itself in a contemporary world that devalues experiences of interiority, depth, and embeddedness in personal history. Psychoanalysis's coming to terms with this modern world—reflected in contemporary relational paradigms and emphases on interaction, authority, and epistemology—is essential yet tends to replace an outdated conformity with an updated one, in which what is offered to analysands may become limited and the soul of psychoanalysis lost.

Bollas's work attempts to reinspire psychoanalysis. This paper explores his contributions and the tensions within them and develops several points about how psychoanalysis can maintain a worthwhile self—for itself and for its analysands—in the modern world. Among the issues discussed are the sense in which an endogenous motivational core associated with an emphasis on interiority may be compatible with a relational paradigm and how the notion of personal idiom is a rich and fruitful one, but that the cultural field deserves a more fundamental place than it is given by Bollas. The problem of authority and exploitation, within and outside the consulting room, is also taken up, and it is argued that psychoanalysis should be conceived as a moral discourse in which the analyst's self-subverting (but not diminished) authority is essential.

responseSet.title: Listening Three Frost Poems
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Listening Three Frost Poems

Thomas Ogden MD

Thomas Ogden

306 Laurel Street
San Francisco, CA 94118

Thomas Ogden  

Joseph Brodsky (1995a) has referred to poetry as “a great disciplinarian” (p. 100) to prose. I would add that poetry is a great disciplinarian to analytic listening. In this paper I look at the way language is used in the making of poetry in three Frost poems. My interest is not so much in what a poem is “about,” but rather in what a poem is. I discuss the ways effects are created in language and how these effects, taken together, create the unique experience the reader generates (with the poet) in listening to the poem. I leave it entirely to the reader to make what connection he or she will between the experience of listening to poetry and the experience of listening to the language created in an analytic relationship.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Can We Use Observations of Infant-Caregiver Interactions as the Basis for a Model of the Representational World?: Commentary on Paper by Beebe, Lachmann, and Jaffe

Peter Fonagy PHD

Peter Fonagy

Gower Street
London WC1E 6BT UK

Peter Fonagy  

The program of work reported by Beebe, Lachmann, and Jaffe is remarkable from the point of view of both its originality and, perhaps more important, its theoretical coherence. It is a highly satisfactory aspect of this work that it raises so many exciting questions for further research. For example, is the lack of exact matching on facial-mirroring tests a predictor of infant security in the strange situation in the manner that temporal or rhythmic aspects of mother-infant or stranger-infant communication was found to be? Or, is the latency of the infant's response to the caregiver in the time-series (Auto-regressive Integrated Moving Average ARIMA) models of predictive significance (was the infant who responded 1 12 seconds after the mother's change of engagement level anxious-avoidant, anxious-resistant, or disorganized)? Can mother-infant pairs be clustered in terms of the ARIMA functions of their interactions; what are the developmental sequelae for a slowly responding infant of a rapidly reacting mother or vice versa? How does such responsivity evolve; is it the same throughout the child's development or does the mother become gradually more sensitive to the infant with development? And can we demonstrate in longitudinal studies the evolution of the schemata of interaction hypothesized by Beebe and colleagues?

Peter Fonagy, Ph.D. is Freud Memorial Professor of Psychoanalysis, University College, London; Research Director, The Anna Freud Centre, London, and Coordinating Director, Child and Family Center, Menninger Foundation, Kansas.

[[RunningHead]]

A Transformational Model of Presymbolic Representations: Reply to Commentaries

Beatrice Beebe PHD , Frank Lachmann PHD and Joseph Jaffe MD

Joseph Jaffe

722 W. 168th St., #108
New York, NY 10032-2603

221 West 82nd Street, Apt. 11G
New York, NY 10024

722 W. 168th St.
New York, NY 10032-2603

Joseph Jaffe  

There of the four discussants have to varying degrees misunderstood our position on presymbolic representations as implying “permanent” (Tabin, Kulka), “entrenched” (Harris), “crystallized” (Kulka) structures, with linear implications for adult development (Harris). We focus first on this issue, and then turn briefly to several of the individual responses of the discussants.

The “Permanence” of Early Interactive Representations

Overall, Kulka has understood extremely well the systems model or inherent field theory underlying the paper: “a system defined by the constant interactive process which exists between its components …” We heartily agree that formulating “interactiveness” as a psychoanalytic concept, a “system whose processes are its essence,” is critical to psychoanalysis. Ironically, Kulka has misunderstood us as well, in characterizing our core idea as “an a priori existence of a permanent interactive system”. The interactive system is not a priori, but emerges as a function of the dyad; it is not “permanent,” but rather is in constant process and potential reorganization.

Harris's description of our argument, that “early pattern becomes long-term, entrenched procedural memory for interaction … reproduced in adult dyadic life” places procedural memories into a linear model. Instead, we have espoused a much more transformational view, suggesting that only in pathology is there a loss of this transformational process.

responseSet.title: Are We To Have a Meeting of Minds?: A Reply to the Discussions of A Meeting of Minds
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Are We To Have a Meeting of Minds?

A Reply to the Discussions of A Meeting of Minds

Lewis Aron ABPP

Lewis Aron

13 Longview Road
Port Washington, NY 11050
Email: lewaron@psychoanalysis.net

Lewis Aron  

It seems quite unlikely that my commentators and I will end this discussion with a meeting of the minds in the sense articulated here by Stolorow to connote agreement and unity of opinion. We are not all going to be of “one mind” about these matters, and that, I believe, is a good thing, for in using the metaphor of a meeting of minds, throughout my book, I tried to be clear to distinguish mutuality from merger or fusion. I do think that as psychoanalytic colleagues we can attempt to understand and even empathize with each other's positions and perspectives on these matters; we may, under the best of circumstances, mutually influence each other so that in a sense we become involved in a “negotiation” of the differences among us, but this hardly requires that we maintain an ideal of mutual consensus, agreement, or lack of conflict. Similarly, in the clinical psychoanalytic situation, the metaphors of mutuality, negotiation, and a meeting of minds are efforts to grapple with our commonality with our patients, our connectedness, and our reciprocal, bidirectional influence, but not to minimize or underplay the important asymmetries, the important differences in our knowledge, power, authority, and responsibilities. In what follows, I try to respond to the numerous criticisms of my work introduced so thoughtfully by the three discussants. I will be neither systematic nor exhaustive, but rather I try to address what seem to me to be central and salient themes.

Lewis Aron, Ph.D., ABPP is a Clinical Professor and Supervisor, New York University Postdoctoral Program and Adelphi University, Derner Institute, Postdoctoral Program; and Associate Editor, Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

© 1997 The Analytic Press

responseSet.title: Ten Years of Doing Psychotherapy While Living with a Life-Threatening Illness: Self-Disclosure and Other Ramifications
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ten Years of Doing Psychotherapy While Living with a Life-Threatening Illness: Self-Disclosure and Other Ramifications

Amy Morrison L.I.C.S.W.

Amy Morrison

Amy Morrison  

Do not ask to know for whom the bell tolls: it tolls for thee.

—John Donne

The author describes the impact on her clinical practice of a 10-year struggle with recurrent breast cancer. Informed by tales of therapists who kept their disease secret and thus deprived patients of the experience of saying good-bye, she explores a process of clinical decision making involving self-disclosure and the clinical indications that patients were ready and wanted to learn about her illness. Nonverbal communications from patients, relevant history of disease in friends and relatives, and parallel experiences with others in their current lives were among the indications cited for self-disclosure about cancer. In clinical vignettes, the author explores her process of deciding who and when to tell, and the subsequent work of helping patients deal with the information and potential loss. She considers as well decisions about accepting new patients, countertransference challenges regarding her illness, and the authenticity that self-disclosure provides in allowing patients to determine for themselves whether or not to continue or terminate treatment.

[[RunningHead]]

On Free-Floating Attention

Robert Langan PHD

Robert Langan

120 East 62nd Street #1B
New York, NY 10021

Robert Langan  

This paper is an experimental effort to invite the reader into an interplay between two modes of comprehension: the one predominantly narrative and emotive, and the other predominantly rational and analytic. The interplay of these modes of writing may (or may not, depending on the reader) amplify the thesis of the paper: that Freud's clinical technique of evenly-suspended attention involves a tension between attentional modes—a paradoxical willing-not-to-will—and this willing unwilling (a kind of free will) undercuts both rational and emotive modes of comprehension, thereby permitting novel prehension of self and other.

[[RunningHead]]

Self Psychology and Projective Identification—Whither Shall They Meet? A Reply to the Editors (1995)

Susan Sands PHD

Susan Sands

1664 Solano Avenue
Berkeley, CA 94707

Susan Sands  

The concept of projective identification continues to be viewed as alien, even dangerous, by self psychologists. Six aspects of self-psychology/intersubjectivity theory are explored in an attempt to understand the presumed incompatibility of self psychology and projective identification: 1) the empathic vantage point; 2) the focus on subjective reality; 3) the emphasis on the analyst's personal contribution; 4) the focus on selfobject experience; 5) the disruption-restoration process; and 6) the defining of transference and countertransference as “organizing activity.” The self-psychological/intersubjective concepts that come closest to describing the phenomenon of projective identification—that is, empathic immersion, affect resonance, and reciprocal mutual influence—fail to capture at least three of its essential elements 1) the patient's persistent, unconscious intent to communicate certain unformulated aspects of self through the other; 2) the analyst's sense of being “taken over” by the patient's experience; and 3) the intensely visceral quality of the analyst's experience. It is argued that self psychology ignores this important form of patient communication to its own detriment and that the concept of projective identification needs to be reformulated in terms that are more experience near to self psychologists. It is suggested that there exists a normal, developmental need, a selfobject need, to communicate intolerable, unsymbolized affective

Susan H. Sands, Ph.D. is on the teaching faculty of the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California (PINC) and the clinical faculty of the Wright Institute. She is also Assistant Clinical Professor, Department of Psychology, University of California, Berkeley.

I wish to thank Dr. L. Bryce Boyer for having taught me how to use my countertransference to understand my patients.

© 1997 The Analytic Press

651

experience through the other's experience—a need that remains more pervasive and intense in some of us than in others—and that the longed-for selfobject response is to have one's communication received, contained, and given back in such a way that one knows the other has “gotten” it from the inside out.

responseSet.title: Old Before Its Time?: Commentary on Kenneth A. Frank's Paper
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Old Before Its Time?: Commentary on Kenneth A. Frank's Paper

Jay Greenberg PHD

Jay Greenberg

275 Central Park West, 1BB
New York, NY 10024

Jay Greenberg  

Relational psychoanalysis is coming of age. Created piecemeal, by authors who had little in common and knew nothing of each other, for many years the new approach addressed a wide range of issues in a cacophony of voices. There was little consensus—even about the problems that needed to be addressed—and absolutely no systemization. Systemization itself was a prime target of many relational thinkers; it had begotten both the abstruse, mechanistic theory of the Freudian ego psychologists and their narrow, rigid canons of technique.

On my reading, Frank's paper signals a change in the way relational analysts are thinking about their project. Frank carves out and holds our attention to what he considers the proper observational field (the totality of the analyst's participation, and especially the analyst's inadvertent participation). Following this, he reaches a technical prescription: the analyst must engage authentically with the patient. Just as Freud defined a subject and a method (the patient's unconscious and the analyst's neutrality), so does Frank. There is a surefootedness and a certainty about his approach that betokens the maturity of the tradition within which he operates. Frank knows what must be looked for and what must be done. This is what I mean by the coming of age of relational thinking, for better and for worse.

I will begin with the better, the suggestions that I find important and useful in Frank's paper. All of these stem from his tenacious commitment to what he considers a two-person model of the psychoanalytic situation and from his rigorous pursuit of its implications.

Dr. Greenberg is Training and Supervising Analyst, William Alanson White Institute, and Faculty and Supervising Analyst, New York University Doctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. He is the author of Oedipus and Beyond (1991) and editor of Contemporary Psychoanalysis.

© 1997 The Analytic Press

[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst's Subjectivity—How It Can Illuminate the Analysand's Experience: Commentary on Susan H. Sands's Paper

Howard Bacal PHD

Howard Bacal

1221 Norman Place
Los Angeles, CA 90049-1540

Howard Bacal  

The objective that Dr. Sands sets for herself in her stimulating paper is to use the experience-near lens of self psychol ogy to illuminate certain aspects of projective identification that self psychology theory has hitherto obscured—what she has elsewhere (Sands, 1995) termed “these more neglected or shunned aspects of the countertransference field.”

One of the great problems about addressing these issues is that there is considerable disagreement about how the terms countertransference and projective identification should be defined; in addition, these terms are frequently used synonymously, or, as I would prefer to put it, they are confused and confounded with one another, as well as with the idea of empathy. Sands, unfortunately but inevitably, inherits these difficulties from both traditional psychoanalysis and self-psychology. I agree with her that it is important to attempt to bring clarity to this situation from a self psychological perspective. I believe that we can do so by thinking about empathy, countertransference, and projective identification—which are central to the issues that Sands addresses in her paper—as aspects of the analyst's subjectivity. Then we can consider what determines it, what it may tell us about the patient, and whether, at any moment, it is a facilitator or disruptor of the therapeutic process.

Dr. Bacal is Training and Supervising Analyst, Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles and Toronto Institute of Psychoanalysis; Southern California Psychoanalytic Institute, Los Angeles; Supervising Analyst, Institute for the Psychoanalytic Study of Subjectivity, New York City. He maintains a private practice in Los Angeles.
