The Patient's Perception of the Analyst's Self-Disclosure: Commentary on Amy Lichtblau Morrison's Paper

Judith Chused MD

Judith Chused

1805 Randolph St., N.W.
Washington, D.C. 20011-5339

Judith Chused  

It is indeed a privilege to discuss Amy Morrison's paper. Her husband, Andy, has been a friend and colleague of mine from the time when he was an admired resident at the “Psycho” (Massachusetts Mental Health Center) in Boston and I was a medical student, just beginning to explore the wonders of the human mind. Andy introduced me to Amy at one of the psychoanalytic meetings in New York, but I never got to know her. Reading her paper, I regret that, because what emerges from the pages is a strong and passionately determined individual, with a capacity for honest introspection that served her and her patients well. She comes across as a therapist who was exacting yet compassionate toward herself; a woman committed to understanding herself and the motives behind her feelings and actions. I believe that in her attempts to understand the complex determinants of her therapeutic actions, Amy Morrison has enriched our field enormously.

Rather than discuss the woman who appears between the pages of the paper, however, I would like to focus on the paper itself and what I found most impressive and valuable, in it—the self-discipline that Morrison both demonstrated and held up as a therapeutic ideal.

Judith Fingert Chused, M.D. is a Training and Supervising Analyst, Washington Psychoanalytic Institute, and Clinical Professor of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences and Clinical Professor of Pediatrics at the George Washington University School of Medicine.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and the Degradation of Romance

Stephen Mitchell PHD

Stephen Mitchell

251 West 71st Street
New York, NY 10023

Stephen Mitchell  

Despite its centrality in the lives of many people, psychoanalysis, with its traditional emphasis on psychosexuality and rationalism, has generally undervalued the importance of love and romance. This essay explores this devaluation within psychoanalytic theorizing and the parallel widespread degradation of love in everyday life. Whereas common wisdom claims that familiarity extinguishes love, I argue that the illusory security that familiarity often masks is endangered by genuine love, with all its accompanying risks.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Paradox of Innocence: Dissociative “Adhesive” States in Perpetrators of Incest

Sue Grand PHD

Sue Grand

764 Wendel Place
Teaneck, NJ 07666

Sue Grand  

This paper attempts to develop a phenomenology of the perpetrator's conviction of innocence in a condition of actual guilt. This phenomenology is developed through the investigation of dissociative states in a certain type of incestuous perpetrator: one who is herself a survivor of sexual abuse, physical abuse, or both. Clinical material suggests that certain types of schizoid perpetrators can genuinely experience the incestuous act as not really real, not really sex, not really mine. This clinical phenomenon is examined from the perspective of two contemporary theoretical trends: the new view of the self as multiple (Bromberg, 1993; Mitchell, 1993), and the neo-Kleinian formulation of “adhesive” (Mitrani, 1994) or “autistic-contiguous” (Ogden, 1989, 1990) modes of pseudo-object relatedness. Incestuous acts are conceived as occurring within a prelinguistic modality, and are therefore not encoded in discursive, autobiographical memory. The coexistence of dissociated, multiple self states and modalities accounts for the contradictor levels of object relatedness, memory, and concern frequently encountered in perpetrators of incest.

responseSet.title: Time and Self: On the Intersubjective Construction of Time
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Time and Self: On the Intersubjective Construction of Time

Beatriz Priel PHD

Beatriz Priel

Beer-Sheva 84105
Israel
FAX: 07-6900046
email: bpriel@bgumail.bgu.ac.il

Beatriz Priel  

This paper develops the idea that the human experience of time is not a creation of an isolated mind, but emerges within the earliest intersubjective experiences. Following Winnicott's main theoretical assumptions about the role of the environment and the processes of ego integration, the paper focuses on the sense and the concept of time as stemming from within a self-with-other context and constituting an emergent property of self-other differentiations. These processes are viewed also from the perspective of the dialogical character of early mother-infant interactions as evidenced by recent infant developmental research. The dialogical structure of these interactions includes important aspects of synchrony and accommodation, as well as asynchrony and transformations, which play a basic role in the emergence of self-differentiation. Moreover, the temporal characteristics of mother-infant exchanges constitute a basic vehicle through which interpersonal meanings unfold. The evolution of the sense of time is seen as a continuous process of construction of meaning in an intersubjective framework. Four basic aspects/phases of this evolution are proposed. These underscore basic features of the experience of time and self-object differentiation, including timelessness or a time of togetherness, the containment of absence or time of separation, narrative time related to the configuration and refiguration of a self in time, and the renunciation of immortality and the “use” of time.

[[RunningHead]]

Principles of Dynamic Systems, Intersubjectivity, and the Obsolete Distinction Between One-Person and Two-Person Psychologies

A Meeting of Minds: Mutuality in Psychoanalysis by Lewis Aron (Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1996)

Robert Stolorow PHD

Robert Stolorow

11726 San Vicente Blvd.
Suite 410
Los Angeles, CA 90049

Robert Stolorow  

A Meeting of Minds is a wonderful book. Aron's style of articulating his ideas is a metalogue of the content he seeks to articulate. The book's subject matter is “relational-perspectivism,” a broad-based postmodern viewpoint in psychoanalysis that has drawn from interpersonal psychoanalysis, British object relations theory, self psychology, and interactionally oriented contemporary Freudian thinking, and that, according to Aron's account, has reached an integration in the teachings of relational psychoanalysts at the New York University postdoctoral program in psychoanalysis. Essentially, the viewpoint holds that clinical phenomena are relationally constituted and that both participants in the analytic relationship have valuable perspectives on its vicissitudes. Aron presents this viewpoint in a manner that mirrors its basic tenets. His theoretical net is inclusive rather than exclusive. He considers the views of others, including those with whom he disagrees, carefully, respectfully, and fairmindedly rather than dismissively.

Robert D. Stolorow, Ph.D. is a faculty member and training and supervising analyst at the Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles, and a faculty member at the Institute for the Psychoanalytic Study of Subjectivity, New York City.

[[RunningHead]]

Analysis as a Mutual Endeavor—What Does It Look Like?

A Meeting of Minds: Mutuality in Psychoanalysis by Lewis Aron (Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1996)

Ruth Stein PHD

Ruth Stein

3 Brody Street
Jerusalem 92502, Israel.
e-mail: rustein@netvision.net.il

Ruth Stein  

Lewis Aron's important book is several things at once. It is a historical narrative of the relational approach as it came into life in the New York University postdoctoral program. It is an appeal to classical analytic thinkers who have changed their views about the analytic procedure to acknowledge and relate to the corpus of interpersonal writing. It alludes to the author's Odyssey, which set sail with a Freudian training until reaching affinities of choice in the interpersonal tradition, silently documenting his experience and ways of working as a clinician. The book is also a strong articulation of relational thinking, where the concept of mutuality is foregrounded and systematically elaborated. Aron's whole way of writing is intensely relational: He relates to origins, other theories, social and cultural streams, and colleagues. There is a strong sense of a polylogue, with numerous addressees in a lively interchange.

Ruth Stein, Ph.D. is member and teacher at the Israel Psychoanalytic Institute, lecturer at the Tel Aviv University Psychotherapy Program, and author of Psychoanalytic Theories of Affect (1991). She is on the Editorial Board of Gender and Psychoanalysis and on the International Editorial Board of the American Journal of Psychotherapy.

An earlier version of this essay was presented at the Annual Meeting of the Israel Association of Psychotherapy, Zichron Yaakov, May, 1996.

responseSet.title: Reply to Carolyn Clement's Commentary
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Carolyn Clement's Commentary

Judith Rustin CSW

Judith Rustin

Judith Rustin  

It is a challenge to respond to Carolyn Clement's extension of my work. Because we work with different sets of assumptions derived from different theoretical models, it at times seems as if we are applying the characteristics of appels to a discussion of oranges. Nevertheless, there are three areas of commonality to which I can respond: (1) her extension of the concept of agency, (2) her critique of the lack of bidirectionality in the clinical material; and (3) her concerns about the clinical material, which I believe is a misinterpretation. I will use the latter two points to raise some larger issues about the way we report clinical material.

Clement extends the concept of agency to self-experience at the motoric level through proprioceptive feedback. She further elaborates agency as the infant's experience of his own states and activities during the “disjoining” periods or periods of “open space.” These aspects of agency are important but significantly different from the aspect of agency that I have highlighted, that is, the contingent responsiveness of the human environment to the infant's regulation of his own state.

I chose to highlight the human environment's contingent responsiveness because (1) it seems to afford a better parallel to the analyst—patient dyad, and (2) I believe it is a foundation from which other capacities develop. For example, the capacity of the infant to experience itself in the alone state is partly a function of the effectiveness of the infant—caregiver system in helping the infant achieve state regulation. The case of Jack is illustrative. Jack's parents were both massively indifferent and psychotic. As an adult, Jack did not experience his own inner states with any specificity. He did not have access to the experience of hunger, fatigue, proprioceptive feedback, or, at another level, basic affect states.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Quantum Selfhood: Commentary on Paper by Beebe, Lachmann, and Jaffe

Raanan Kulka MA

Raanan Kulka

Ramat Hagolan 7
Ramot Eshkol
Jerusalem 97 703
Israel

Raanan Kulka  

The quest to establish an objective foundation for the enigmatic issues of selfhood and relations between selves places infant research at the center of the courageous effort to resolve ontological problems using an epistemological body of knowledge.

Beebe, Lachmann, and Jaffe's scholarly paper is an instructive example of the revolution taking place in infant research, a revolution inspired by the evolution of psychoanalysis from its previous stages of energy and structure to its ontological stage of experience (Kulka, 1988).

The authors' core idea is of the a priori existence of a permanent interactive system between the infant and his mother in which the experiences of self, other, and self-with-other come into being. This notion receives an astonishing empirical verification in this paper through an abundance of observational and experimental results, all of which convincingly show that the unfolding of the human infant's immeasurable potential is not the product of an autonomous development but is the actual process of the mother-infant dyad itself.

The authors' claim that psychoanalysis has not yet recognized the concept of a systemic dyad as an essence in and of itself is most perspicacious, and the possibility of formulating interactiveness as a psychoanalytic concept would indeed be an achievement. The conception of human interaction as an entity defined not by its components—the self and the other—but by the process of its own interactiveness necessitates a deviation from the Newtonian worldview on which psychoanalytic metapsychology is based.

Raanan Kulka, M.A. is Training and Supervising Analyst at the Israeli Psychoanalytic Society and Clinical Director of Milgo, Institute of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy for Children and Adolescents, Jerusalem.

@ 1997 The Analytic Press

responseSet.title: The Emergence of an American Middle School of Psychoanalysis: Commentary on Karen Rosica's Paper
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Emergence of an American Middle School of Psychoanalysis: Commentary on Karen Rosica's Paper

Charles Spezzano PHD

Charles Spezzano

2252 Fillmore Street
San Francisco, CA 94115

Charles Spezzano  

Karen Rosica raises for us a critical issue in analytic work, those moments in the transference-countertransference situation when one participant becomes a voice within the other rather than a voice from outside. Rosica hears a voice coming out of her (“Are you afraid that I cannot bear the burden of your loving me?”) and wonders whose it is. In her wondering she offers us an elegantly simple definition of countertransference: the analyst doesn't sound like herself to herself. She has become momentarily unrecognizable to herself. The voice has left out part of Rosica's understanding of the patient's state of mind: the disappointment and loss that lie behind her representation of Rosica as unable to bear the burden of her love. The voice has also violated a technical rule of practice in which Rosica believes: don't abruptly interpret an account of an extratransferential event as the manifestation of a latent narrative about oneself, especially not for the purpose of communicating reassuringly that you are not being the way the patient experiences you as being but have been mistaken for someone else, in this case the mother who cannot tolerate bad feelings in her daughter. The issue immediately engaged is: might this be anxiety of the patient communicated unconsciously to the analyst and symbolized in her consciousness through the question with which Rosica opens her essay?

Charles Spezzano, Ph.D. is Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California. He is the author of What to Do Between Birth and Death: The Art of Growing Up (1993) and Affect in Psychoanalysis: A Clinical Synthesis (The Analytic Press, 1993).

[[RunningHead]]

Defects in the Self: Liberating Concept or Imprisoning Metaphor?

George Atwood PHD and Robert Stolorow PHD

Robert Stolorow

New Brunswick, NJ 08903

Robert Stolorow  

The doctrine of defects in the self leads to a clinical focus on what is missing rather than on what is present in pathological organizatons of self-experience. Felt deficiencies in the sense of self may be understood not as manifestations of an irreducible internal defect within the person, but rather as the consequence of various organizing principles established in early transactions with caregivers. An illustration of this idea is provided by a brief description of the life and treatment of a young woman who felt that she had no self and that she did not exist.
